text:
Phenolic compounds represent a large group of plant secondary metabolites that may bind to proteins and affect their structure and activity. Protein-phenolic interaction has become a subject of increasing interest due to its contribution to human health and food industry. These complexes are established via either covalent or non-covalent bonds like hydrophobic and ionic interactions, hydrogen and van der Waals bonds. The present review summarizes the mechanisms of their interaction as well as the related physiological effects and their applications in food industry.

Different classes of polyphenols (depending on their concentration, molecular weight and specific structural features as well as environmental factors such as pH and cofactors) can interact with various proteins which could lead to either soluble or insoluble protein–polyphenol complexes by protein folding or unfolding depending upon their nutritional, functional and biological properties. Molecular docking and simulation studies have become valuable tools for in silico virtual screening of diverse polyphenols to explore their binding affinity and sites on the target protein molecules. This could help in understanding competitive and non-competitive protein–polyphenol conjugation toward identifying potential active ingredients of nutraceuticals and functional foods. This chapter provides a general overview into insights derived from in silico docking studies on flavonoids and their possible interaction with milk proteins using such potential bioactive nutraceuticals and functional foods as examples, in conjunction with their experimental in vivo studies, which could bridge the gaps between the different biochemical events associated with food digestion in the digestive tract. It would also enable the design of new bioactive food additives and ingredients that provide enhanced therapeutic applications.
